title: The Warehouse Fire
summary: A business owner is charged after a failing warehouse burns down days before an insurance deadline.

preview.summary: A business owner is charged after a failing warehouse burns down days before an insurance deadline.
preview.charge: A business owner is accused of orchestrating a warehouse fire for financial gain.
preview.evidence: The case turns on insurance timing, surveillance gaps, and testimony about the owner’s recent conduct.
preview.complication: The building also had electrical faults and multiple people with access knew the site and alarm routines.

policeReport: Fire investigators concluded the blaze began near stacked solvent drums in the rear loading bay. The owner had increased coverage the week before, but inspectors also documented long-standing electrical faults and inconsistent alarm maintenance.

witnesses.name: Dana Pierce
witnesses.statement: The burn pattern concentrated in the loading bay and did not match a simple wiring fire. I cannot exclude deliberate ignition, but the damaged wiring complicated the final origin assessment.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Technical and credible, but careful not to overstate certainty.

witnesses.name: Troy Holland
witnesses.statement: The owner talked for weeks about being crushed by inventory losses and insurance deadlines. He also ordered valuable stock moved off-site two days before the fire.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Has direct operational knowledge, but left the company after a conflict over severance.

witnesses.name: Leila Nasir
witnesses.statement: I warned the warehouse twice that the rear panel was overheating and could fail under heavy load. The repairs were scheduled but had not been completed before the fire.
witnesses.reliabilityNotes: Supports an accidental-fire theory, though she did not inspect the scene after the blaze.

evidence.description: Coverage was increased by 35 percent four days before the fire.
evidence.relevance: Supports motive, but can also reflect legitimate business risk planning.

evidence.description: Exterior loading-bay cameras were offline for 47 minutes during the likely ignition window.
evidence.relevance: Creates uncertainty around who accessed the loading bay before the fire spread.

evidence.description: High-value stock was moved to a secondary site two days before the blaze.
evidence.relevance: Could suggest planning, or a routine response to storage and cash-flow issues.

priorRulings.title: People v. Mireles
priorRulings.relevance: Insurance motive alone is insufficient without evidence connecting the accused to the fire-setting act itself.

prosecutionObjective: Establish that the owner prepared financially for the fire, protected valuable stock in advance, and exploited the surveillance gap to arrange a deliberate burn.
defenseObjective: Demonstrate that the fire origin remains uncertain, electrical defects provide a credible accidental cause, and the prosecution cannot bridge the gap between motive and actual ignition.
